summary: This post reflects on Martin Fowler's experience at OOPSLA 2005, covering various sessions and developments in software engineering. He highlights the Subtext project for its innovative approach to non-text-based programming, discusses emerging concerns about concurrency with multicore processors, and notes advances in refactoring tools. Fowler muses on OOPSLA's identity crisis now that object-orientation has become mainstream, questioning whether the conference still has purpose. He concludes that while OOPSLA may seem directionless, its value lies in its community of practitioners who continue exploring new frontiers beyond OO advocacy.
key_points: Subtext and Language Workbenches represent a shift away from text-based source code toward abstract representations projected for editing
Concurrency is becoming a critical concern with multicore processors, requiring language-based approaches that ordinary developers can handle
Refactoring tools are evolving to use type inference and can automate code migration between library versions
Teaching programming through debugging and refactoring existing code may be more effective than writing from scratch
OOPSLA has transitioned from an advocacy conference to a community gathering, having won the battle for object-orientation's dominance
money_quotes: Just because you think you can prove that the opposition can't score a goal is no reason to not play a good goalkeeper
Dynamic types are stronger than static types, as they don't flee the field at runtime
Static types give me the same feeling of safety as the announcement that my seat cushion can be used as a floatation device
Eclipse has a 'guess the button' user interface
only 12 times faster than C means you haven't started optimizing
key_insight: Long-running technical communities derive their value not from their original mission but from the ongoing conversations and new explorations of the practitioners they've brought together.
